text: The LORD kept his word and took note of Sarah as he had promised.
Sarah conceived and bore Abraham a son in his old age, at the exact time God had said.
Abraham named the son Sarah bore him Isaac.
Abraham circumcised Isaac on the eighth day, just as God had commanded.
Abraham was a hundred years old when Isaac was born.
Sarah said God had made her laugh, and everyone who heard would laugh with her.
She said no one would have told Abraham that Sarah would nurse children, yet she had borne him a son in his old age.
The child grew and was weaned, and Abraham held a great feast on the day Isaac was weaned.
Sarah saw the son Hagar the Egyptian had borne Abraham mocking.
She told Abraham to send away the slave woman and her son, since he would not share the inheritance with Isaac.
This troubled Abraham greatly because of his son Ishmael.
God told Abraham not to be distressed and to do as Sarah said, since Isaac's line would carry his name.
God said he would also make a nation of Hagar's son, since he too was Abraham's offspring.
Early the next morning Abraham gave Hagar bread and a skin of water and sent her and the boy away, and she wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba.
When the water ran out, she left the boy under a bush.
She sat at a distance, unable to watch him die, and wept aloud.
God heard the boy crying, and an angel called to Hagar from heaven, telling her not to be afraid.
The angel told her to lift the boy up and hold him, for he would become a great nation.
God opened her eyes and she saw a well of water, filled her skin, and gave the boy a drink.
God was with the boy as he grew up, living in the wilderness and becoming a skilled archer.
He settled in the wilderness of Paran, and his mother got him a wife from Egypt.
At that time Abimelech and his army commander Phicol came to Abraham, noting that God was clearly with him in all he did.
Abimelech asked Abraham to swear he would deal fairly with him and his descendants, as Abimelech had dealt kindly with him.
Abraham agreed to swear the oath.
Abraham then complained to Abimelech about a well that Abimelech's servants had seized.
Abimelech said he had not heard of it until that day.
Abraham brought sheep and oxen and gave them to Abimelech, and the two made a covenant.
Abraham set apart seven ewe lambs from the flock.
Abimelech asked what the seven lambs meant.
Abraham said accepting the lambs would serve as proof that he had dug the well.
So the place was named Beersheba, because there the two of them swore an oath.
After the covenant was made at Beersheba, Abimelech and Phicol returned to Philistine territory.
Abraham planted a tamarisk tree at Beersheba and called on the name of the LORD, the Everlasting God.
Abraham stayed in the land of the Philistines for a long time.
